**DATA ENGINEERING HACKATHON**

**On-Premise IoT Data Migration to AWS Cloud**

**Instructor \- Qasim Hassan**

# **Project Overview**

This hackathon simulates a real-world enterprise scenario: a company runs IoT sensors in an on-premise data center. The sensors generate continuous geolocation and telemetry data stored in a PostgreSQL database. Your challenge is to design and implement a complete AWS-based data pipeline that ingests this IoT data, migrates it to the cloud, processes it, and serves it through analytics and reporting tools.

 

**PHASE 1** 

 

![][image1]

 

 

 

**PHASE 2**

 

![][image2]

**Architecture Summary**

| Data Source | IoT Sensors → AWS IoT Device Simulator → MQTT → AWS IoT Core |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Ingestion** | Kafka MSK (iot-events topic) → Kafka Connect JDBC Sink → PostgreSQL EC2 |
| **Backup** | Kafka S3 Sink Connector → Amazon S3 Backup Bucket |
| **Migration** | Debezium CDC → Kafka MSK → Snowflake Kafka Connector → Snowflake Bronze |
| **Processing** | dbt (SQL models) → Snowflake Silver → Snowflake Gold |
| **Analytics** | Snowflake Gold → Streamlit Dashboard |
| **Time-series** | AWS Timestream → Grafana Visualization |

 

 

  **PHASE 1  —  IoT Ingestion & On-Premise Simulation** 

# **Phase 1: IoT Data Ingestion Pipeline**

In Phase 1, you will simulate an on-premise data center using AWS services. IoT devices are replicated using the AWS IoT Device Simulator, and their real-time data is ingested through AWS IoT Core into Apache Kafka on AWS MSK. Kafka Connect uses a JDBC Sink Connector to write events directly into PostgreSQL running on EC2 — simulating an on-premise server. A Kafka S3 Sink Connector provides the optional backup path.

 

## **Phase 1 — Architecture Flow**

IoT Sensors (Simulated)  →  AWS IoT Core (MQTT)  →  Kafka MSK (iot-events topic)  →  Kafka Connect JDBC Sink  →  PostgreSQL EC2  \+  Kafka S3 Sink  →  S3 Backup

 

## **Phase 1 — Tech Stack**

| Component | AWS Service / Tool |
| :---- | :---- |
| **IoT Simulation** | AWS IoT Device Simulator (geoLocation data, O2 Arena London) |
| **Message Protocol** | MQTT via AWS IoT Core |
| **Stream Delivery** | Apache Kafka — AWS MSK (replaces Kinesis Firehose) |
| **DB Writer** | Kafka Connect — JDBC Sink Connector (replaces Lambda MySQL writer) |
| **On-Prem DB** | PostgreSQL on EC2 — private subnet (replaces MySQL) |
| **Security** | AWS Secrets Manager (DB credentials), VPC, Bastion Host |
| **IaC** | AWS CDK (Python) — provisions MSK, Kafka Connect, S3, VPC |
| **Backup** | Kafka S3 Sink Connector → Amazon S3 backup bucket |
| **Access** | AWS Systems Manager Session Manager (no public SSH) |

 

## **Phase 1 — Tasks**

### **Task 1.1 — AWS Account & Environment Setup**

●       Create AWS account and apply best practices (MFA, billing alerts, IAM roles)

●       Install and configure AWS CLI with programmatic access

●       Set up AWS CDK (Python) and install all dependencies

●       Create AWS MSK (Kafka) cluster — note the bootstrap broker URLs

### **Task 1.2 — IoT Device Simulator**

●       Deploy the AWS IoT Device Simulator

●       Create a device template for geoLocation data (latitude, longitude, timestamp, device\_id)

●       Start simulation of 5+ virtual devices in parallel

●       Create an IoT Core Rule that routes MQTT messages to Kafka MSK topic iot-events

### **Task 1.3 — Kafka MSK & Kafka Connect**

●       Deploy Kafka Connect cluster connected to MSK

●       Configure JDBC Sink Connector: topic \= iot-events, target \= PostgreSQL EC2

●       Store PostgreSQL credentials securely in AWS Secrets Manager

●       Optional: configure Kafka S3 Sink Connector for backup to S3

### **Task 1.4 — PostgreSQL EC2 (On-Prem Simulation)**

●       Launch an EC2 instance in a private subnet (no public IP)

●       Install PostgreSQL and create the IoT schema and tables

●       Enable WAL (Write-Ahead Log) — required for Debezium CDC in Phase 2

●       Set up a Bastion Host EC2 for secure administrator access

●       Use AWS SSM Session Manager to connect — no direct SSH key required

### **Task 1.5 — CDK Deployment**

●       Write CDK stacks for: MSK cluster, Kafka Connect, S3 bucket, VPC \+ subnet

●       Deploy with cdk deploy and verify all resources created in Console

●       Run the full simulation end-to-end and confirm data reaches PostgreSQL on EC2

 

 

 

  **PHASE 2  —  Cloud Migration, Processing & Analytics** 

# **Phase 2: CDC Migration, Snowflake & dbt Analytics**

In Phase 2, Debezium CDC captures every change from PostgreSQL in real time — replacing AWS DMS. Change events stream through Kafka MSK into Snowflake (replacing AWS RDS) via the Snowflake Kafka Connector. Inside Snowflake, dbt (replacing AWS Glue) runs SQL-based Bronze → Silver → Gold transforms. A Streamlit dashboard queries the Gold layer, and a parallel path writes to AWS Timestream for Grafana time-series visualization.

 

## **Phase 2 — Architecture Flow**

PostgreSQL EC2 (WAL)  →  Debezium CDC (Kafka Connect source)  →  Kafka MSK (cdc.public.iot\_events)  →  Snowflake Kafka Connector  →  Snowflake Bronze

Snowflake Bronze  →  dbt silver model  →  dbt gold model  →  Streamlit Dashboard

Snowflake  →  AWS Timestream  →  Grafana (time-series charts)

 

## **Phase 2 — Tech Stack**

| Component | AWS Service / Tool |
| :---- | :---- |
| **CDC / Migration** | Debezium CDC via Kafka Connect (replaces AWS DMS) |
| **Event Transport** | Kafka MSK — carries CDC change events to Snowflake |
| **Data Warehouse** | Snowflake — Bronze / Silver / Gold layers (replaces AWS RDS) |
| **Transforms** | dbt — SQL models for Silver and Gold layers (replaces AWS Glue) |
| **BI Dashboard** | Streamlit — queries Snowflake Gold layer |
| **Time-Series DB** | AWS Timestream |
| **Visualization** | Grafana (replaces QuickSight for time-series) |
| **Language** | Python, SQL, dbt (Jinja SQL) |

 

## **Phase 2 — Tasks**

### **Task 2.1 — Debezium CDC Setup**

●       Verify PostgreSQL WAL is enabled: wal\_level \= logical on EC2

●       Deploy Debezium PostgreSQL connector inside Kafka Connect

●       Configure: source \= PostgreSQL EC2, output topic \= cdc.public.iot\_events

●       Verify CDC events appear in Kafka topic (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE events)

### **Task 2.2 — Snowflake Setup \+ Kafka Connector**

●       Create Snowflake account (free trial), database HACKATHON\_IOT

●       Create schemas: RAW (Bronze), CLEAN (Silver), ANALYTICS (Gold)

●       Deploy Snowflake Kafka Connector — consume cdc.public.iot\_events topic

●       Verify Bronze table RAW.IOT\_EVENTS is receiving CDC records

### **Task 2.3 — dbt Project**

●       Install dbt-snowflake, configure profiles.yml with Snowflake connection

●       Write silver model: validate nulls, parse timestamps, add severity tags

●       Write gold model: group by device\_id \+ date, compute daily aggregates

●       Run dbt run and dbt test — all models must pass

●       Generate dbt docs and review the Bronze → Silver → Gold lineage graph

### **Task 2.4 — Streamlit Dashboard**

●       Connect Streamlit to Snowflake Gold layer using snowflake-connector-python

●       Build 3 charts: device activity map, time-series AQI trend, top-N devices

●       Dashboard must auto-refresh every 30 seconds

### **Task 2.5 — Timestream \+ Grafana (Bonus)**

●       Write a Lambda that reads from Snowflake and writes to AWS Timestream

●       Connect Grafana to Timestream and build a live sensor panel

 

 

 

 

# **Submission Requirements**

●       GitHub repository with all code (CDK stacks, Kafka Connect config, dbt models, Streamlit app)

●       Architecture diagram covering both phases (PNG or PDF)

●       Screenshots of every deliverable listed above

●       README.md with setup steps and how to run the full pipeline

●       5-minute demo: show live CDC event flowing from PostgreSQL to Snowflake Gold

 

 

**Good luck — build it end to end\!**
